questionText: You arrive at a cell site and measure a bus voltage of 58.2 V during normal float. The plant controller shows no alarms. Battery room temperature is 42°C. Temperature compensation is enabled with a coefficient of -4 mV/cell/°C. What is the most likely explanation for the elevated bus voltage?

choices.text: The batteries are fully charged and the voltage is within normal tolerance
Temperature compensation is functioning incorrectly — at 42°C the voltage should be lower than normal float, not higher; investigate the probe seating and compensation circuit
The rectifiers have entered equalize mode, which is normal on a hot day
A battery string has failed open and the voltage reflects only the rectifier output

explanation: At 42°C (17°C above 25°C reference), temperature compensation should REDUCE float voltage by 4 mV/cell × 24 cells × 17°C = 1.63 V, putting the bus below normal float. A voltage of 58.2 V (well above standard 54 V float) with compensation enabled indicates a probe or compensation circuit failure — the system is running high instead of low.

questionText: While installing a new circuit at a BDFB under live-bus conditions, a technician has connected the negative conductor to the load frame and is about to connect the positive return conductor to the bus bar. The correct next action is:

choices.text: Connect the return conductor quickly to minimize exposure time
Insulate all adjacent lug positions on the bus bar, verify polarity with a meter at both ends, then connect the return conductor using an insulated-handle tool while maintaining one-hand contact
Shut down the rectifiers first so the bus is at a lower energy level
Have a second technician hold the conductor in position while the first technician bolts the lug

explanation: Live-bus lug work requires: insulate adjacent positions to prevent accidental shorts, verify polarity before connecting (reversed polarity destroys equipment), and use insulated tools with one-conductor-at-a-time discipline. Shutting down rectifiers does not de-energize the bus (batteries still hold it). Two-person free-hand contact on a live bus increases arc risk.

questionText: A 200 A cell site has a 4-hour battery reserve design. During last month's 90-minute utility outage, the LVD operated at the 70-minute mark. The battery strings were last capacity tested 3 years ago. The most accurate conclusion from these facts is:

choices.text: The LVD threshold is set too high and should be adjusted downward
Battery capacity has degraded: the site discharged to LVD threshold in 70 minutes vs 240 minutes design, indicating approximately 29% of rated capacity remains — strings likely need replacement
The site load grew beyond design — a load audit is needed before condemning the batteries
The utility outage must have been accompanied by an AC anomaly that damaged the rectifiers

explanation: 70 min / 240 min design = 29% of rated capacity. Three-year-old VRLA strings without recent capacity testing are a common finding for this level of degradation. While a load audit is good practice, the severity of the discrepancy (71% capacity loss) points strongly to battery degradation as the primary factor. LVD threshold adjustment would be wrong — it would allow deeper discharge of already degraded strings.
referenceBookSection: Module 16 — Cell Sites, Huts & Outside Plant Power

questionText: A new technician with an AC background asks why equipment chassis at a -48V plant are bonded to the DC bus return conductor instead of a separate equipment ground wire. The correct explanation is:

choices.text: It is a cost-saving measure — fewer conductors are needed

In positive-ground architecture, the DC return (+48V side) IS bonded to building ground; the equipment chassis bonded to the return IS on the safety ground — there is no separate equipment ground because the return conductor serves both functions
The chassis must be isolated from ground to prevent corrosion
Equipment at telecom sites does not require grounding because the voltage is below 50V

explanation: Positive-ground architecture: DC return (positive side of the -48V plant) is bonded to earth at the main ground bar. Every equipment chassis bonded to that return is also on earth ground. The return IS the safety ground conductor. AC technicians must unlearn the AC model (separate equipment ground) for DC plant work.

questionText: After installing a replacement rectifier brick, you clamp-meter the eight bricks in the shelf and find: bricks 1-7 each output 12 A, brick 8 (the replacement) outputs 0 A. The most likely cause and correct action are:

choices.text: The replacement brick has a higher output voltage — reduce its setpoint

The replacement brick is contributing 0 A, which indicates its OR-ing circuit failed on insertion, a configuration mismatch, or a firmware incompatibility — check the brick's part number against the shelf specification and firmware version
The load is already covered by the other seven bricks, so 0 A on the new brick is correct
All bricks should be replaced simultaneously to avoid this imbalance

explanation: A newly installed brick contributing 0 A has not integrated into current sharing — either an OR-ing failure on insertion, a voltage regulation mismatch, or a firmware incompatibility. Part number and firmware must match the shelf specification. The other bricks covering the load does not explain a working brick showing zero contribution.

questionText: A BDFB fuse for a critical radio frame clears during a storm. Before installing the replacement fuse, the most important diagnostic step is:

choices.text: Check the weather report to see if lightning caused a transient
Measure resistance between the load circuit conductors and between each conductor and the DC return to detect a short before energizing the replacement
Install a larger fuse to handle any future transients
Reset the plant controller to clear any fault logs

explanation: A fuse that cleared once during a storm may be a legitimate transient — but before replacing it, measure the load circuit for shorts (conductor-to-return and conductor-to-conductor). A short blows the replacement immediately and can damage distribution hardware. The check takes 60 seconds and prevents a cascading event during an already stressed site.

questionText: A carrier's SLA requires 8 hours of battery reserve at a 150 A DC load site. The design efficiency factor is 0.8. What minimum battery Ah capacity is required?

choices.text: 960 Ah

explanation: Reserve Ah = load (A) × hours ÷ efficiency factor = 150 A × 8 h ÷ 0.8 = 1,500 Ah. The 0.8 factor accounts for discharge losses and aging margin — without it you would be undersizing the actual delivered capacity.

questionText: A millivolt-drop hunt at a 300 A bus section finds 30 mV between the MBDFB bus bar and the BDFB input terminal. The next measurement is 5 mV between the BDFB input terminal and the load frame terminal. Where is the primary resistance, and what is the repair action?

choices.text: The resistance is in the BDFB-to-load-frame cable; replace the cable

The 30 mV drop between MBDFB and BDFB (R = 30mV/300A = 0.1 mΩ) localizes to that cable run or connection — retorque all lugs in that section and inspect for damaged cable; the 5 mV drop in the BDFB-to-load section is within acceptable range
Both sections have acceptable drop — 30 mV total is within tolerance
Replace the entire BDFB panel — distributed resistance across a panel is not serviceable

explanation: The millivolt-drop hunt narrows resistance by measuring between sequential equal-potential points. 30 mV at 300 A = 0.1 mΩ, which is elevated for a bus bar connection or short cable run — the fault is in the MBDFB-to-BDFB section. The 5 mV drop in the next section is proportionally lower and likely acceptable. Start with retorquing all lugs in the high-drop section before replacing cable.

questionText: During a prolonged utility outage, the site batteries have been discharging for 25 minutes. The site generator is running but the ATS has not transferred. The NOC shows the plant in 'battery-only' mode. The field tech's first action at the site is:

choices.text: Disconnect the battery strings to preserve them from deep discharge
Read the ATS controller display and event log — determine if the ATS fault is a failed transfer command, a voltage sensing issue, or a mechanical interlock problem before intervening
Manually connect the portable genset to bypass the ATS
Contact the utility company to restore power first

explanation: Logs first. A running generator with a failed ATS transfer has several potential causes — a miswired transfer command, a voltage or frequency sensing fault, or a mechanical issue. Reading the ATS controller display takes 2 minutes and identifies the correct fix. Bypassing the ATS without understanding the fault can create a dangerous parallel-source condition.

questionText: During a storm recovery, you receive two dispatch orders simultaneously: Site A has LVD operated 15 minutes ago (loads already lost); Site B has its generator running but shows 55 minutes of battery remaining with the generator fuel level at 30% (about 2 hours of fuel). Which site do you go to first, and why?

choices.text: Site B — preventing a second outage is more valuable than recovering an already-failed site

Site A — a dark site is actively causing a network outage and must be restored first; Site B has 55+ minutes of battery plus generator time, and the NOC can coordinate fuel delivery to Site B while you restore Site A
Flip a coin — they are equivalent priority
Site B — the generator fuel problem will cause a second outage before you can return

explanation: Storm triage: dark sites (LVD operated, loads lost) are first priority — they represent active network failure. Site B has a time buffer: 55 minutes of battery plus 2 hours of generator fuel = nearly 3 hours of margin. The NOC can coordinate fuel delivery during that window. Site A has been dark for 15 minutes with no buffer — restore it first.
referenceBookSection: Module 16 — Cell Sites, Huts & Outside Plant Power

questionText: The -48V negative-ground convention in telecom power was originally chosen because:

choices.text: 48V is the lowest voltage that can power telephone switching circuits
Galvanic corrosion attacks the positive conductor; negative ground directs corrosion toward the return/ground path, protecting the current-carrying negative conductors feeding equipment
It was mandated by the FCC in the 1960s as a safety standard
Negative voltage prevents lightning strikes from reaching equipment

explanation: The Bell System made this engineering decision in the 1880s-1900s when telephone exchanges ran on wet-cell batteries in building basements. Galvanic corrosion attacks the positive electrode; negative-ground makes the return/safety-ground the sacrificial path, protecting the load-current conductors.

questionText: The plant controller has been scheduling equalize cycles every 45 days. A new tech asks what equalize does. The correct explanation is:

choices.text: Equalize disconnects one battery string at a time to test each independently

Equalize runs the bus at a higher voltage (2.33-2.40 V/cell) for a defined period to bring all cells to full charge and reduce sulfation — the controller manages it on a schedule per the battery manufacturer's specification
Equalize balances current between rectifier bricks
Equalize runs when bus voltage drops below the LVD threshold to charge the batteries faster

explanation: Equalize is a controlled high-voltage charge cycle that corrects cell-to-cell charge imbalances and reduces sulfation in VRLA strings. It runs at 2.33-2.40 V/cell (vs 2.23-2.27 V/cell for float) on a controller-scheduled interval of 30-90 days.

questionText: The LVD relay operates at a cell site during a 3-hour utility outage. Two non-critical loads (HVAC and lighting) lose power; the radio equipment remains on. This is:

choices.text: A plant malfunction — the LVD should not operate during a 3-hour outage on a 4-hour reserve site
The designed function of LVD — sacrificing non-critical loads (HVAC, lighting) when bus voltage falls to threshold, to preserve battery runtime for critical revenue loads (radios)
Evidence that the battery strings are completely failed
A safety trip to protect personnel from low-voltage hazards

explanation: LVD is designed exactly for this scenario: when bus voltage falls to threshold (~42V), it disconnects non-critical loads to extend runtime for critical equipment. HVAC and lighting being shed while radios stay on is the correct, designed behavior. On a 4-hour reserve site discharging to LVD at 3 hours, battery capacity is within range but warrants a capacity test to verify margin.

questionText: After replacing a failed rectifier brick and verifying normal bus voltage and current sharing, the field tech's next professional obligation is:

choices.text: Nothing — the plant controller will auto-report the repair to the NOC
Contact the NOC to confirm the alarm has cleared at the monitoring system and provide a repair summary — failure to close the loop results in duplicate dispatches
Submit a written report within 48 hours to the carrier's engineering team
Leave a handwritten note on the plant controller for the next tech

explanation: NOC loop closure is a professional basic in telecom power. The monitoring system may not auto-clear after physical repair; if the NOC still shows the alarm open, a duplicate dispatch will be generated. The field tech confirms the repair and alarm clearance to the NOC directly.
referenceBookSection: Module 16 — Cell Sites, Huts & Outside Plant Power

questionText: A plant has three parallel battery strings. Clamp-metering each string's fuse under load shows: String 1: 85 A, String 2: 88 A, String 3: 22 A. The most likely diagnosis for String 3 is:

choices.text: String 3 is the newest string and contributes less until it ages to match the others
String 3 has elevated internal resistance — likely aging cells — causing it to contribute only 25% of its peers' output; it will likely fail the next capacity test
String 3's fuse is partially blown and should be replaced
This is normal variation — string currents do not need to be balanced

explanation: Strings 1 and 2 share load equally at ~86 A each; String 3 contributes only 22 A — approximately 25% of the others. In a healthy parallel string configuration, contributions should be within ±10-15%. The most common cause of a significantly undercontributing string is elevated internal resistance from aging or failing cells. This is an early indicator that String 3 will fail the next capacity test.

questionText: A DC power plant technician is dispatched to a rooftop cell site in Manhattan to service the DC plant in the equipment hut. The work requires accessing the area adjacent to the antenna array to trace a cable run. Before proceeding, the technician must:

choices.text: Verify that hard hats are available — the primary hazard near antennas is falling objects
Confirm RF status and ensure RF safety clearance before approaching the antenna array — RF energy at cell site power levels creates thermal burn risk at close range
Nothing additional — the low voltage of the DC plant means the site is safe
Only RF tower climbers need RF safety clearance; ground/hut technicians are exempt

explanation: RF thermal burn risk applies anywhere near active transmitting antennas, including rooftop and antenna-adjacent work areas. DC power work is normally confined to the equipment hut (outside the RF zone), but any work that takes a tech near the antenna array requires RF safety clearance regardless of the work being performed.
referenceBookSection: Module 16 — Cell Sites, Huts & Outside Plant Power

questionText: A technician who has completed DC plant training is interviewing with a tower company (not a carrier). The tower company's DC power tech role focuses on:

choices.text: Maintaining the carrier's radio and network equipment on the tower
Maintaining the shared site infrastructure — the DC power plant, batteries, shelter, grounding, and generator — which all carrier tenants depend on, independent of any carrier's equipment
Installing new 5G radios for the carrier tenants
Managing the NOC alarm queue for the tower company's portfolio

explanation: Tower companies are neutral infrastructure hosts — they own the tower, shelter, grounding, and increasingly the on-site power plant. Their power tech role covers the shared infrastructure that supports all carrier tenants. The carrier tenants maintain their own radio and network equipment.

questionText: A DC plant is designed N+1 with 6 total rectifier bricks, each rated 1.5 kW. The site load is 7.5 kW. During your PM visit, one brick shows fault status. The correct action is:

choices.text: The site is in a degraded state but stable — the remaining 5 bricks can carry 7.5 kW (5 × 1.5 kW = 7.5 kW); replace the faulted brick at the next scheduled maintenance
Replace the faulted brick during this PM visit — N+1 means one spare; with one already failed, any second failure will shed load. The site is now N+0 and the spare must be restored immediately
Add a seventh brick to maintain N+1 integrity
Reduce the site load to 6 kW to match available capacity

explanation: N+1 with 6 bricks means: 5 carry the 7.5 kW load (5 × 1.5 kW = 7.5 kW) with 1 spare. With 1 failed, the site is still running but has lost its redundancy margin — any second failure now sheds load. The failed brick must be replaced immediately during this PM visit to restore the N+1 posture. Waiting for 'next maintenance' is incorrect risk management.

questionText: A field tech arrives at a site during a utility outage with a portable generator. The site's permanent generator failed to start. The batteries have 90 minutes of reserve remaining. Before connecting the portable genset to the site's genset receptacle, the first step is:

choices.text: Start the portable genset and connect it while it's running at low RPM to reduce the startup surge
Verify the utility disconnect is open before connecting the portable genset — this prevents backfeed that could energize utility lines and endanger line crews restoring the outage
Disconnect the battery strings to reduce the load the portable genset must start with
Connect the portable genset while the permanent generator is still turning (even though it won't start) to attempt a parallel start

explanation: Utility disconnect open = no backfeed risk. This is the non-negotiable first step for any portable generator connection — regardless of whether the site has an interlock. A portable genset backfeeding a utility line can kill the line crew working to restore the outage. Verify the disconnect is open independently before proceeding.
referenceBookSection: Module 16 — Cell Sites, Huts & Outside Plant Power

questionText: A technician completing this DC Plants course asks how this credential relates to a carrier's site access qualification program. The correct relationship is:

choices.text: This credential replaces carrier qualification programs — no additional training is needed

This credential establishes foundational DC plant knowledge that applies across all platforms; carrier qualifications are additional, platform-specific authorizations layered on top of this foundation
Carrier qualifications are only required for tower climbers — DC plant techs do not need them
This credential and carrier qualifications are unrelated — carrier programs do not recognize external training

explanation: Foundational credentials (this portal) and carrier-specific qualifications serve different purposes. The foundation builds transferable technical knowledge across all DC plant platforms. Carrier qualifications (AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile vendor programs) authorize work on specific equipment in specific carrier environments. Both are needed; neither substitutes for the other.
